title: Ecotourism company makes the Green List
summary: GIRDWOOD -- When Kirk Hoessle of Alaska Wildland Adventures discovered his company had been chosen as the highest-ranking outdoor tour operator in the world by a high-end glossy travel magazine this fall, he thought, "Wow. That's cool." Then he got back to work. Others aren't quite so nonchalant. They say that Wildland Adventures' winning Conde Nast's 11th annual Green Award as ecotourism guide of the year says a lot about changes happening in Alaska's outdoor-travel industry. Today, people want more out of their vacations, local experts say. Alaska's greatest asset remains its outdoors splendor, but it's no longer enough to load throngs of people aboard a bus for a sightseeing drive. Outdoor tourism is changing. People aren't as driven to go combat fishing for that big king salmon if they…

title: Unusual Turnagain Arm tide has attractions and dangers
summary: Imagine driving south on Saturday for a perfect spring picnic on Turnagain Arm. Shake off that winter weariness, pack a baguette and a jar of Nutella, and find a quiet overlook where you can toss out a blanket and watch the sun sizzle snow off the mountains. It's a relaxing picture, but there's one more reason to make the trip this weekend. Grab your binoculars, and for goodness' sake, stay out of the water. The new moon is here, and it's bore tide time. Like an army marching 25 miles up Turnagain Arm, the bore tide advances in one cascading wave -- a wave that can reach 6 feet high and carry surfers for miles. The Arm, with its shallow, narrow, gently sloping basin, is perfect for this rare natural phenomenon. And when you shove a huge volume of water into this basin twice a day -- Turnag…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Stranded climbers rescued MOUNT HUNTER: High winds pinned Korean mountaineers. By TATABOLINE BRANT Anchorage Daily News Published: May 28, 2006 Last Modified: May 28, 2006 at 03:22 AM Two Korean climbers were rescued Saturday evening after being stranded near the top of a mountain in Denali National Park for more than 24 hours without food, water or sleeping bags. Whang Cheung Sik and Hong Sung Hyun, both 35, were stuck near the 14,000-foot level of Mount Hunter, according to a statement released by park officials. Rangers learned of the situation around 7:30 p.m. Friday. Details were sketchy because of limited radio communication. As far as officials could initially piece together, the two men were ascending the technically challenging "Deprivation" route on the 14,573-foot Mount Hunter, …

title: Questions arise over rescue of two on Flattop
summary: Lost on the slopes of Flattop Mountain April 27, Evan Alvarez and Nicholas Loutzenheiser did what any sensible young men would do: They called mom for help. By then, their evening snowboard adventure on the slopes of the distinctive and oft-climbed mountain above Anchorage had gone bad. The duo ended up tired and bewildered on the "other side of Flattop,'' as 22-year-old Alvarez put it. And what side would that be? "The side not facing the parking lot,'' he said. He thought maybe it was the east side facing Powerline Pass. Chugach State Park rangers say it was the west side facing Rabbit Creek. On the east side, 1,500 feet or so below the summit, is a heavily traveled trail -- the Powerline Pass Trail -- that leads back to the Chugach parking lot at Glen Alps. On the west side about 2,000 …

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Has drought, flood of pink salmon stymied Kodiak's Buskin River silver salmon run? By CRAIG MEDRED Anchorage Daily News Published: October 2, 2005 Last Modified: October 2, 2005 at 01:35 AM Ankle-deep in the carcasses of dead and dying pink salmon along Kodiak Island's Buskin River, biologist Len Schwarz searched for the coho salmon that weren't there and wondered what had gone wrong. Most years, September brings a flood of the silver-bright, hard-bodied salmon to the Buskin. Anglers who have discovered the bounty fly in from all around, grab their gear and make the short walk from the Kodiak airport to the Buskin River Inn to prepare for action. Around the middle of September each year, the hotel at the edge of the airport boasts some of the best just-out-the-door angling in the world. Bu…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Baiting is traditional form of bear hunting Published: July 6, 2003 Last Modified: July 13, 2003 at 03:41 AM Old pilots make me uncomfortable. Old people tend to forget things. Forgetting things is not a good idea when you are at the controls of an airplane. So let's ban old pilots in Alaska? How about an initiative to make it illegal for anyone older than 60 to fly an airplane in the 49th state? And what about big-game guides? I don't much like them either. They are in business almost solely to cater to rich folk. Why should the rich be able to buy this service? Shouldn't they have to work hard at learning to hunt like everyone else? So how about a ballot initiative banning big-game-guiding in the 49th state too? I bring these examples forward to illustrate just a couple of things I don't…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Biologists blame wolves for herd's lack of growth CARIBOU: Fortymile numbers plateau; expanded wolf hunts are considered. By TIM MOWRY Fairbanks Daily News-Miner Published: April 30, 2006 Last Modified: April 30, 2006 at 03:46 AM FAIRBANKS -- The Interior's largest caribou herd has stopped growing, and state wildlife officials say wolves are to blame. A multipronged recovery plan may have helped the Fortymile caribou herd nearly double in size over the past eight years. But now the herd's size has plateaued around 43,000. "The growth of that herd has stopped, and the reason is more animals are dying," said information officer Cathie Harms with the Alaska Department of Fish and Game in Fairbanks. "We're relatively confident that the increase in mortality is due to predation." In response, t…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Backpack Boating Water no hazard to backcountry hikers carrying inflatable rafts By Roman Dial Special To The Daily News Published: September 16, 2001 Last Modified: September 23, 2001 at 03:25 AM By the time Dave Manzer and I reached the east bank of the glacial Skilak River in Alaska's first adventure race -- the Hope to Homer of 1982 -- we'd hiked and jogged for 24 hours, covering more than 50 miles. Then we faced our first real challenge. Fast flowing gray silt powered two-foot waves that crested far apart, a sign of fast, deep water. Upstream we could see the Skilak Glacier tumbling into an iceberg-choked lake. The far side of the river, where we wanted to be, was about four lanes of highway and a median strip distant. To get across, we'd have to swim. We looked at each other. The loo…

title: Kenai college opens fishing academy this summer
summary: So you've always wanted the perfect excuse to give your significant other when you wanted to spend a few summer days on your favorite river fishing. How about this: "It's a college class." It's not a tall tale or a fantasy. Thanks to the Kenai Peninsula College, you can legitimately claim you're enhancing your education while fishing this summer. The college is offering the Kenai Fishing Academy for the first time beginning in June. Weeklong courses will be offered on fly-fishing and hard-tackle fishing. "We were looking for some classes that would interest people up here in the summertime," said Dave Atcheson, evening coordinator at the college and coordinator of the fishing academy. "(Gary Turner, college director) was interested in a hands-on class. He wanted people to really know how t…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: New outdoors gear reveals scant innovation RETAILER SHOW: Big event in Salt Lake City offers little to crow about. By RYAN JORDAN Special to the Daily News Published: April 16, 2006 Last Modified: April 16, 2006 at 04:53 AM Editor's note: Amid the "revolutionary'' glitz and "all new'' glam of the gear being unveiled out at the winter Oudoor Retailer show in Salt Lake in January, backpackinglight.com publisher Ryan Jordan found too little new. He was left wondering when outdoor gear became more about fashion than performance. His report: Outdoor Retailer Winter Market 2006 has finally come to a close, and not a moment too soon. More than any Outdoor Retailer show I've attended, this one was uneventful -- whether your outdoor passion runs to skiing, snowshoeing, backpacking or hiking. Manufa…

title: Invitational riders find McGrath and their own limits
summary: In the waning daylight hours of Feb. 26, three figures trudged through fresh, thigh-deep snow as they climbed Rainy Pass in the Alaska Range. Each pushed a gear-laden mountain bike. Peter Basinger, 25 and from Anchorage, was far ahead, just in sight, breaking trail. Rocky Reifenstuhl, 55 and from Fairbanks, and Jeff Oatley, 36 and Reifenstuhl's neighbor, followed, marveling at Basinger's endurance. Reifenstuhl and Oatley figured Basinger would burn himself out, allowing them to catch up. Basinger didn't burn out. But before this race was over, the three men would push themselves to a level of exhaustion few people experience. The human-powered Iditarod Trail Invitational does this, reducing competitors to mad, vomiting, hallucinating lumps of soul-weary, blistered flesh. It is a race from …

title: Alaska sports digest: Weaver leads silver salmon derby
summary: Anchorage's Rodney Weaver Jr. grabbed the Downtown Soup Kitchen Slam'n Salm'n Derby's first-day lead Friday in the pro class with his 11.15-pound fish caught in Ship Creek. Weaver reeled in his fish around 4 p.m., while second place angler Jioji Lino of Anchorage caught his 9.45-pounder earlier in the day. Derby board member Marty Turnbow said low tide will be a good tide for anglers this week. "The bulk have been coming in at low tide," he said. "The fishing is going to get better as the week progresses. "It's all about the tide." Today's low tide is at 3:44 p.m. The derby, sponsored by GCI and the Alaska Railroad, raises money for the Downtown Soup Kitchen, a shelter for Anchorage's homeless. Earlier this summer, the King Slam'n Salm'n Derby raised $12,500, according to Turnbow, which pa…

title: Ron Wilmot: Details make a difference when you're on the river
summary: At most combat fishing holes, there always seems to be one person who keeps hooking fish after the action slows. Other anglers -- particularly those casting for hours with nothing to show for it -- find this is annoying. What's he doing that I'm not? Is he snagging? Some might note the lure being used. Chances are it's the same lure everyone else is using. The difference is likely in how it's being used. The more I fish, the more I realize how seemingly insignificant factors can mean the difference between going home without a bite and catching your limit and a tale or two of a big one that got away. Some recent fishing trips confirmed this. Variables such as time of day, stream water levels, how hard to set the hook, how much weight to use and where to place it on the line are important f…

title: Front runners out of the gate
summary: They come in all sorts of shapes and sizes, with varied temperaments and dispositions. Some are slow and methodical. Others are fast and frenzied. But one thing can be said about any good lead dog: They are worth their weight in gold. Ask anyone who runs dogs to tell you about their leader and their face will soften, a smile usually appears. You'll hear adjectives like "smart," "solid" and "strong." "Chaika has no fear," says recreational musher Dr. Lynn Hornbein of one of her Samoyed leaders. "She barrels ahead at full speed. She is not fazed by any terrain or obstacle." Egil Ellis usually spots them early. "The first time they get hooked up, you can see they are more confident than the other puppies and they are really focused from the first time in harness," says champion sprint musher …

title: 1 of 1 A wolf-dog hybrid wearing, according to protesters, a bulletproof vest, howled during a demonstration held by wol…
summary: 1 of 1 A wolf-dog hybrid wearing, according to protesters, a bulletproof vest, howled during a demonstration held by wolf advocates in Anchorage. A national animal rights group will no longer hold "howl-ins," a grass-roots campaign launched two years ago in which hundreds of protests were staged nationwide in a failed attempt to convince Alaska's governor that killing wolves is wrong. Photo by AL GRILLO / Associated Press archive 2003

title: Winter kings relieve 'graydoms'
summary: When the Winter Wonderland that has been Alaska for hundreds of years becomes slushy, muddy and gray like a far-north Seattle suburb, what's an outdoors man or woman to do? Go fishing, of course. That's one Puget Sound solution to the graydoms, a mood disorder brought on by gray skies meeting a gray landscape. A segment of the Northwest population has long treated this ailment by heading for a boat, firing up the motor, grabbing some rods and looking for feeder king salmon. Over the past decade, as the Alaska climate has warmed, more and more Alaskans have discovered that what works in Puget Sound also works in Cook Inlet, Kachemak or Resurrection bays, and off Kodiak Island. Jeff Peterson of Kodiak Combos, based out of Old Harbor on Kodiak Island, found the perfect way to brighten up a re…

title: Sight fishing at its finest (10-25-2005)
summary: CLEARWATER CREEK -- Unloading an 18-foot canoe at the boat ramp drew looks from the local motor-heads. Maybe it was the fact that we were attached to the only vehicle in the parking lot with individual rear tires instead of dualies. Or maybe it was that we were heading out for an overnight float in October, when the forecast high temperature was projected to stay below freezing. Regardless, we donned waders and loaded gear while other anglers watched from inside the comfort of down parkas, truck engines at idle, perplexed looks on their faces that seemed to say one thing: casualties. Little did they realize, but the dry ground under our felt-bottomed boots was far more favorable to a fishing trip than the snow-dumping blizzard I had experienced here two years before. Those were true winter…

title: Fishing report:  Mountain lakes offer beautiful scenery and fat fish
summary: RABBIT LAKE -- The trip combined rainbow trout fishing and thin mountain air with some of the most marvelous scenery on Earth. Along the lake's eastern shore -- where fish rose among the reflections of sheer rock walls and alpine tundra -- it was easy to pretend that you were, as the late journalist Charles Kuralt once wrote, "alone in the universe." And yet, on the Fourth of July, you were not alone. Not entirely. That's because this high-country lake, tucked in a Chugach Mountains amphitheater backed by the 5,000-foot-tall Suicide Peaks, is among Anchorage's most popular hiking destinations. Beautiful as any far-flung Brooks Range or Wrangell Mountains lake, Rabbit Lake and several other backcountry tarns -- some containing grayling, rainbow trout, Dolly Varden and arctic char -- are a s…

title: Class instructs amateurs on basics of angling and stewardship
summary: COOPER LANDING -- Curt Muse stood on the cobbled shore of Quartz Creek, casting a 3-weight fly rod upstream as a dozen middle-age-or-beyond students watched. The longtime guide spotted a sockeye salmon, red as a fire hydrant but easy to miss as it swam above colored rocks and below a surface rippled like glass on a shower stall. "You can barely see that fish, and he's red," Muse said. "There could be trout near by him." He counseled students to cast first in shallow water rather than splashing to midstream. His made his point the fifth time his nymph drifted down by hooking an 8-inch Dolly Varden, a char found in the same cold streams as trout. Muse was the day's guest lecturer for the Kenai Fishing Academy, a week-long class offered four times per summer by Kenai Peninsula College, a bran…

title: No, kayak anglers aren't crazy -- they're stable
summary: For the past six years, Christopher Mautino has had to convince people that fishing from a kayak is not as crazy as it might sound. Sure, kayaks are small, Alaska water is cold and the fish are big. The combination sounds wacky. But last week, as he reeled in a small halibut in Resurrection Bay while dangling his feet over the edge of his specially designed fishing kayak, the moment was incredible. Forget boats with loud motors creating big wakes. With little more than the sound of his line being reeled in, fishing from his kayak was a peaceful experience. Mautino owns Liquid Adventures, a Seward-based kayak tour company that also offers guided fishing trips from kayaks built especially for the sport. Most fishing is in water 20 feet to 200 feet deep for halibut, salmon or rockfish. "A mis…

title: To catch trout, have nose for salmon (7-28-2005)
summary: Some of the best-looking trout water produced absolutely nothing earlier this week. Glassy pools with roiling heads and swift tail-outs resembled images from classic fly-fishing paintings, but many offered no fish. Veteran angler Paul Cyr was not surprised. Nor was he concerned. He waded upstream, methodically peering into each slick and eddy for a telltale sign. And when he finally found it 100 yards upstream of where he'd started, he stopped short like a bird dog on point, crouched slightly and started casting. The spot, a piece of water hardly larger than a dining room tabletop, seemed an unlikely choice. The current was fast, part of a sequence of rapids that dashed over a channel of pumpkin-size stones. It offered no slack water for fish to rest, no alders or undercut banks for cover.…

title: King salmon set to swarm Deshka River
summary: Jim Platz and his buddies live on the Kenai River, home of the largest king salmon ever caught. But on Monday, they were on the Deshka, convinced they'd have better luck king salmon fishing in Mat-Su. King salmon fishing is heating up on the Deshka, where the Alaska Department of Fish and Game, in anticipation of a big run, has upped the bag limit to two kings per day and four in possession. Bait is allowed too. As of Monday, though, only 1,811 fish had passed through the weir seven miles upstream, and anglers along the river groused that they weren't having much luck. By Tuesday, though, the count was up to 3,000 and gaining momentum. The peak is coming. Ray Blodgett, captain of Fisherman's Choice Charters, said he expects the best fishing this week, as kings start moving through en masse…

title: Seward harbor teems with eager silver prize anglers
summary: SEWARD -- For about three hours Saturday, Teresa Dalebout's 3.44-pound silver was the No. 1 salmon of the longest-running fishing derby in Alaska. The Alaska Seward Silver Salmon Derby turns 50 this year, and Dalebout of Soldotna was there to try her luck. Her first catch, the toy-sized silver she pulled in amid gauzy early-morning fog, is one she would've thrown back most days. But on opening day of the derby, she knew she might be the first one to turn in a fish, thus earning $100. She was. "It made me nervous out there, though," she said. "You couldn't see 20 feet in front of the boat. You can't see the (other) boats. It's dangerous. I'm going to use the $100 to buy a nice compass for the boat." Derby committee chairman Dean Kasischke said the dark morning caused a slow start to the der…

title: The Anchorage Daily News (www.adn.com)
summary: FISHING Learn to tie your own flies for free at clinic Alaska Flyfishers hosts a free fly-tying clinic from 10 a.m. to 4 p.m. today at the Municipal Animal Control Shelter, 4711 Bragaw St. Bring tools and a vise if you have them. If not, the club has items to lend. Come and go at any time throughout the day. There will be several instructors on hand, and participants are encouraged to work with each to learn different patterns and techniques. For information, call 561-8330 or just show up. LECTURES Tsunamis are topic of Fireside Chat series Come learn more about the powerful waves of tsunamis at 7 p.m. Wednesday at the BLM Campbell Creek Science Center as part of the monthly Fireside Chat series. Bruce Turner of the West Coast/Alaska Tsunami Warning Center will speak on the forces that gen…

title: Marshall motors away with big prize in derby
summary: One happy Seward Silver Salmon Derby angler will be driving the city streets in style, thanks to his luck on the water. Lynwood Marshall of Anchorage pulled in tagged silver salmon No. 31 on Sunday to win a 2005 Ford Explorer XLS 4X4 sport utility vehicle worth $25,000 in the 50th Seward Silver Salmon Derby. The winner of the first Seward Silver Salmon Derby in 1956 won a Chevrolet Bel Air. Marshall could not be reached Tuesday afternoon for comment; apparently he was still out fishing. The derby, in its fourth day, continued to see the fish approach the 17-pound mark. As of Tuesday afternoon, a Bear had caught the biggest fish entered in the Seward Silver Salmon Derby. Not a four-legged bear, of course, but, hey, a Bear is a bear. They're all natural-born fishermen, right? Ted Bear of Sol…

title: The Anchorage Daily News (www.adn.com)
summary: Backcountry SAFETY First Responder class registration has begun Wilderness Medical Associates will conduct an 80-hour Wilderness First Responder class in Anchorage Feb. 3-12, over two three-day weekends and evenings. The price is $550, which includes all books, lab materials and certifications. To register, e-mail debajango@att.net or call Michael Crotteau at 646-4811. BOATING Kayaking skills class offered at Service High Alaska Kayak School offers a skills course today at the Service High pool. Learn and practice introductory and intermediate kayaking skills. Beginners meet 11 a.m.-2:30 p.m. The intermediate group meets 3-7 p.m. To register, go to REI, call the kayak school at 1-907-235-2090 or go online to www.alaskakayakschool.com . 13-lesson boating skill and seamanship class "Boating …

title: Kenai faces trashy situation once dipnetters head home
summary: KENAI -- For the final three weeks of July, hordes of dipnetters from Anchorage and the Mat-Su area camped out on Kenai's beaches to take part in a fishery that's grown into Alaska's premier fish harvesting frenzy. Now that fishing is over, however, an ugly reminder of the dipnetters' presence remains behind -- trash. Kenai Parks and Recreation director Bob Frates said the city usually spends about a week using city workers to get the majority of trash off the beach and out of parking lots. "It's a good four-day project," Frates said Wednesday. Frates said crews worked Tuesday on the south beach, then moved to the north beach on Wednesday. There, city workers were busily collecting bags of trash, including old shoes, used fireworks and camping supplies, and more. "A couple weeks ago we fou…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Red-hot Russian opens sanctuary early (6-16-2005) Lots of fish opens the sanctuary month early KEN MARSH FISHING REPORT Published: June 16, 2005 Last Modified: December 20, 2005 at 06:31 AM Click to enlarge RUSSIAN RIVER -- Red salmon surging into the Russian River in huge numbers have prompted fisheries managers to open the downstream sanctuary to fishing beginning 6 a.m. today. Even better, biologists believe a strong run of Russian River-bound reds is poised to provide excellent angling for at least two weeks. None of which would have surprised the anglers -- or bears -- who formed a relatively light turnout here Monday morning. "The reds are definitely in," said first-time Alaska angler Tim Ott of Sonoma, Calif., as he stood over his limit of dime-bright salmon in the Russian River Cam…

title: Regulators present revised plan for pollution in spawning areas
summary: JUNEAU, Alaska (AP) - Alaska water regulators have retracted a proposal to lift a statewide ban on pollution mixing zones in fish spawning areas. But under a new proposal, industry could seek exemptions to the rule. The new proposed regulations, out for public comment until Dec. 19, replaces the Alaska Department of Conservation's proposal last year to allow industries and sewage plants to use mixing zones in spawning areas of streams and rivers, which drew heavy criticism. Mixing zones are areas in a water body where pollution discharges exceeding water quality standards are allowed to blend and dilute with clean water. They are permitted by state law in areas outside of spawning zones. They are used by nearly 300 wastewater treatment plants, numerous seafood processors and three mines, i…

title: Fishing for fun: Kachemak Bay's waters are full of halibut and salmon
summary: The five-hour, 225-mile drive south from Anchorage to Homer is more scenic pleasure than long haul. Time passes quickly in a collage of mountain ranges, hanging glaciers, alpine passes and wildlife. And along the way, anglers' hearts beat a little faster as the Sterling Highway crosses the famous Kenai, Ninilchik and Anchor rivers. Then, in its final miles, the road swings west toward the edge of a high bluff to reveal a stunning view of one of Southcentral's crown jewels: Kachemak Bay. A mountain-flanked region of cold, clean marine waters and remote beaches, Kachemak not only promises the fishing trip of a lifetime, it delivers. For most Kachemak Bay anglers, the real fun begins at the end of the road -- in Homer, the small port city located at the Sterling's southern terminus. A sprucy …

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Lethargic fish, chilly weather don't dampen enthusiasm BY CRAIG MEDRED ANCHORAGE DAILY NEWS Published: May 14, 2006 Last Modified: May 15, 2006 at 02:49 AM LONG LAKE -- The muddy trail from the parking lot at the end of the rough, unmaintained gravel road in Palmer's Kepler-Bradley Recreation Area led straight to a milling school of rainbow trout thick as fish in a hatchery holding pond. Inches from the shoreline in water only a foot deep, they circled over and under the detritus of winter, the remains of a plastic milk jug on the bottom, a plastic water bottle bobbing on the surface. Dozens of trout finned out of sight 10 feet offshore, only to circle back so close they almost brushed the grass along the bank. Along the flanks of some fish were bright red slashes indicative of spawning ad…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Silver rushing in tailrace makes for fishy surprise EARLY: Guide had been looking into rumors that kings can show up in April. By CRAIG MEDRED Anchorage Daily News Published: May 3, 2006 Last Modified: May 4, 2006 at 04:33 AM ANCHORAGE -- A quest for the legendary stream salmon of April ended Thursday for fishing guide Andy Couch of Palmer, and then things got a little weird. What Couch had hoped to document was that king salmon -- the big fish of late May and June in Southcentral Alaska -- sometimes get ahead of themselves in the rush to procreate. It had long been rumored that an occasional king shows up in area waters as early as April when shore ice still clings to stream banks and the snow has yet to melt fully from the woods. And the fish Couch pulled from the Eklutna tailrace along …

title: Silvers swarm waters (8-9-2002)
summary: They're aggressive, hard fighting and -- best of all -- they're back. Silvers are the last, best salmon of our summer, living proof that Southcentral's August clouds do indeed have precious linings. Right now, the spunky game fish are flooding into area waters from Resurrection Bay outside of Seward to Clear Creek in the Susitna Valley. "Cottonwood Creek's just full of silvers right now," said Dave Rutz, a state sportfish biologist in Palmer. "And Eklutna (tail race) is phenomenal." That's just for starters. In the Susitna Valley, dime-bright fish have arrived in the Parks Highway streams from Willow Creek north to the Susitna River bridge, Rutz said. Anglers are also catching them in the Deshka River, Lake Creek and Yentna River tributaries. Closer to home, you can't beat Ship Creek. Some…

title: Keep those rods handy; silvers make their debut (8-8-2003)
summary: SUSITNA LANDING -- Southcentral's long summer days are gone, and the nights are growing cooler. Swallows fled south a week ago, and the king salmon runs of June and July are memories. But anglers shouldn't trade in their fishing tackle for skis quite yet. In fact, many will tell you that some of the season's best fishing has just begun -- especially if you're into silver salmon, the fish du jour of August. From Resurrection Bay to the Susitna Valley, these hard-hitting, tail-walking sportfish have begun their late-summer charges up area streams. Often aggressive and quick to strike lures, baits and flies, silvers are local favorites. And few know how to catch them more consistently than longtime angler Don Sherwood of Anchorage. When the silvers started rolling late last week at the conflu…

title: Beads don't fly with some fishermen (7-9-2005)
summary: The fish were there Tuesday, just as I expected they would be. Under a hot sun glaring from the cloudless Susitna Valley sky, you could see their scarlet shapes glowing near the bottom of deep pools. Those fish, of course, were spawning king salmon. Happy as I was to see them, salmon weren't what I sought. Like many Southcentral Alaska anglers who will soon find themselves caught between the closure of the Parks Highway king salmon fisheries on July 13 and the second run of Russian River reds, I was interested in fish of another color. Before long, I found them. Here and there, among those hulking, florid bodies were a bunch of smaller, steel-colored silhouettes. These were rainbow trout and arctic grayling, as inevitable as the spawning salmon they shadowed. As always, catching them would…

title: Cool off and find solitude with small fish close to town (8-20-2004)
summary: E ven as the city broiled in 80-degree heat, the creek bottom Tuesday was cool, shaded by alder, devil's club and a forest of tall birch and spruce. The stream was low, as most are in Southcentral, but in the foothills of the Chugach Mountains the current remained reasonably swift, hissing and tumbling among fern-topped stones. King salmon run in this upper stretch of Campbell Creek, and although the heavy, florid fish of July have long spawned out and died, experience suggested that the stream's resident trout and Dolly Varden would still strike anything that resembled a salmon egg. I rigged up with a bead roughly the size and color of an Iliamna Pinkie and stepped into the creek. Earlier in the day, I'd spoken with local angler David Hagen, who had just returned from a few days of fishin…

title: Whittier's fish still hopping (9-24-2004)
summary: W HITTIER -- Smitty's Cove, a sleepy boat launch on the far edge of town, awoke with a start shortly after dawn Tuesday. That's when the first silver salmon struck a chrome-bladed, green-bodied spinner and commenced to tear the place apart. For no good reason, I'd come under-gunned. The ultralight rod and reel I held and the six-pound-test line connecting me to the dynamo rolling out front were designed for small trout or grayling, not 8- and 10-pound salmon. But at that moment, as the first beams of sunlight spread across the mountains looming over Passage Canal, there was no backing down. All I could do was hang on and let the fish wear itself out. Luckily for me, it played right into my hand. Just when salmon fishing seems to have gone the way of another Southcentral Alaska summer, Whit…

title: Find their food, you'll find the fish (9-3-2004)
summary: I knew we'd found them when I topped a logjam and saw Paul Cyr standing in the stream below, shaking his head. He shouted over the current, "I just lost the biggest rainbow trout of my life." Cyr held his hands apart and stared wistfully at the three feet of empty space between them. Then he gathered his line, checked his leader and resumed casting. Over his shoulder, a good, hard cast downstream, salmon could be seen spawning in the shallows, their tails and dorsal fins breaking the water like miniature sharks. Beyond them the clear stream moved swiftly, cooled and slightly swollen by the first rains of fall. All together, the scene formed a trout angler's nirvana; all of the ingredients for some of Southcentral Alaska's finest fishing were there. Over the next few hours Cyr would catch m…

title: Chugach director brings sea of knowledge to job
summary: For the first time since the 1970s, the Alaska Division of Parks and Outdoor Recreation has reached outside of its inner circle of expertise to fill one of its top positions, that of superintendent of Chugach State Park. Last week, the division announced that the job goes to Cmdr. Tom Harrison, a U.S. Coast Guard officer whose experience in marine safety gives him some natural-resources knowledge but whose years heading large-scale programs gives him the management skills parks officials were looking for. Chris Degernes, the division's chief of field operations, said while it is unusual for hires of such status to be so far-reaching, she feels confident Harrison will excel at the job. He will be managing the third-largest state park in the country and arguably the most popular park in Alas…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Invitational riders find McGrath and their own limits IDITAROD TRAIL: It's a 350-mile trek from Knik. By RON WILMOT Anchorage Daily News Published: March 12, 2006 Last Modified: March 12, 2006 at 12:42 AM In the waning daylight hours of Feb. 26, three figures trudged through fresh, thigh-deep snow as they climbed Rainy Pass in the Alaska Range. Each pushed a gear-laden mountain bike. Peter Basinger, 25 and from Anchorage, was far ahead, just in sight, breaking trail. Rocky Reifenstuhl, 55 and from Fairbanks, and Jeff Oatley, 36 and Reifenstuhl's neighbor, followed, marveling at Basinger's endurance. Reifenstuhl and Oatley figured Basinger would burn himself out, allowing them to catch up. Basinger didn't burn out. But before this race was over, the three men would push themselves to a leve…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: CRAIG MEDRED Stock up on ammo, folks, the bird flu's comin' Published: April 2, 2006 Last Modified: April 2, 2006 at 04:55 AM Stock up on ammo, folks, the bird flu's comin'. The federales are gearing up a $4 million program to find it in Alaska. None of the bird authorities seem to have any doubt that it will get here sometime, someway, somehow. The latest virulent strain -- H5N1, made obvious by the thoroughness with which it decimates chicken farms -- has already spread from Southeast Asia to Asia to the Middle East to North Africa and Southern Europe and now from Southern Europe into Northern Europe and from Northern Africa into most of that continent. Can we be far behind? If it doesn't get here this year, it's sure to arrive next year or the year after or the year after that. Alaska i…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Field notes Published: April 23, 2006 Last Modified: April 23, 2006 at 04:04 AM BIRDING Join Audubon Society for Palmer Hay Flats outing Delesta Fox will lead the annual Palmer Hay Flats Bird Walk at 9 a.m. Saturday. An abundance of waterfowl, shorebirds, cranes and swans gather in the flats before continuing on with their migration to breeding grounds further north. Meet at 9 a.m. on Trunk Road across from the Experimental Station for car pooling. Bring binoculars, scopes and a field guide. Knee or hip boots are necessary to navigate Cottonwood Creek and other areas with standing water. Bring lunch too. Call Fox at 258-6425 or go to www. anchorageaudubon.org . BOATING Registration is under way for sea kayak symposium The 17th-annual Alaska Sea Kayaking Symposium is May 12-14, and registra…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Cold weather, cabin fever bring out anglers in search of big fish on Big Lake Story and photos by ELIZABETH MANNING Daily News correspondent Published: February 26, 2006 Last Modified: February 26, 2006 at 06:07 AM BIG LAKE -- Ice fishing is slow today on Big Lake, to say the least. A good day on the lake might bring up four or five fish compared to the bucket-loads some fishermen pull out of Southcentral's stocked lakes. So why go? So you can watch the ball game on a television inside a fishing shack without having to stop to actually catch a fish? No, of course not. The real answer: big fish. By ice fishing standards, the wild arctic char of Big Lake can be lunkers. The biggest known char pulled from the lake in winter, according to Three Rivers Fly and Tackle in Wasilla, weighed a whopp…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Yakutat welcomes those searching for the perfect wave By MELISSA LARSEN The New York Times Published: April 23, 2006 Last Modified: April 24, 2006 at 03:11 AM YAKUTAT -- "I should get on with you," said a female flight attendant with a wink as I boarded a plane headed from Anchorage to Yakutat. "You're the only woman on here." I smiled and waited for men with salt-and-pepper hair and bellies hanging over their belts to load fishing rods into overhead bins and take their seats. The man I ended up next to smelled of pipe tobacco and engine oil. He was a local from Cordova who told me about a Yakutat bartender who once blew away a patron with a shotgun for being "annoying." It happened about 10 years ago; I can ask anybody, he said. So what's a nice young lady like me doing in a place like th…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Fledgling artists show flair on stamps NATIONAL CONTEST: The youth event moved to Anchorage this year. By MELISSA DeVAUGHN Anchorage Daily News Published: April 16, 2006 Last Modified: April 16, 2006 at 03:13 AM Children's artwork adorned the judges' table last week at the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service offices in Anchorage as the 2006 Junior Duck Stamp Contest began. The 9-by-12-inch pieces showed ducks, geese and swans in various positions on ponds, in the grass or taking flight across blue skies. The best artwork would ultimately move to the national competition, in which young artists from across the United States vie for the chance to have their art on a real U.S. stamp. But at that moment, the judges' work had just begun. They examined each of the 250 or so entries in the Grades K-3 …

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: CRAIG MEDRED Questions arise over rescue of two on Flattop Published: May 7, 2006 Last Modified: May 7, 2006 at 03:41 AM Lost on the slopes of Flattop Mountain April 27, Evan Alvarez and Nicholas Loutzenheiser did what any sensible young men would do: They called mom for help. By then, their evening snowboard adventure on the slopes of the distinctive and oft-climbed mountain above Anchorage had gone bad. The duo ended up tired and bewildered on the "other side of Flattop,'' as 22-year-old Alvarez put it. And what side would that be? "The side not facing the parking lot,'' he said. He thought maybe it was the east side facing Powerline Pass. Chugach State Park rangers say it was the west side facing Rabbit Creek. On the east side, 1,500 feet or so below the summit, is a heavily traveled tr…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Fishing for money Tagged halibut await anglers when Homer derby starts Monday By CRAIG MEDRED Anchorage Daily News Published: April 30, 2006 Last Modified: May 4, 2006 at 04:54 AM ON THE COOK INLET WHALE BITE -- With nine halibut caught, tagged and gently released back into the cold waters of Cook Inlet to await the May start of the Homer Jackpot Halibut Derby, captain Pete Wedin leaned back against the rail of the Julia Lynn and captured the essence of the day. "Now I remember why I don't like to fish halibut in April,'' he said. "My fingers are cold.'' Snow lashed across the aft deck. The Julia Lynn, caught between a brisk wind and an outrushing current, rolled aggressively on her beam between incoming Gulf of Alaska swells. From the comfort of the heated cabin, the VHF radio crackled wi…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Rangers ticket off-limits snowmachiners at Denali National Park $150 FINE: Two groups of riders caught in wilderness zone of 2 million acres. By PETER PORCO Anchorage Daily News Published: April 27, 2006 Last Modified: April 27, 2006 at 03:07 AM Two snowmachiners have been ticketed for illegally riding in an off-limits area of Denali National Park earlier this month in separate incidents, the National Park Service said Wednesday. Each of the riders was the apparent leader among a group of riders, said park spokeswoman Kris Fister. Rangers saw the groups riding in the park's wilderness zone, she said. Thomas Conley of North Pole was cited during the weekend of April 8-9, and Pierre Stragier of Anchorage was cited on the following weekend, Fister said in a phone interview. The citation carri…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Still-water paradise Lakes offer plenty of early-season, late-season action By DAVE ATCHESON Daily News correspondent Published: May 4, 2006 Last Modified: May 4, 2006 at 04:54 AM After the previous day, portaging and paddling through a wind-driven rain, I'm slow to emerge from the warmth of my sleeping bag. Yet I notice wisps of quiet sunshine marbling the walls of my tent, telling me that I have my wish -- a dawn in which the clouds have given way to the pale blue of a clear sky and curtains of fog waltz over the mirror of this glorious tree-lined stage, perfectly set and unbroken. Once up, it's difficult to contain my excitement. Setting out in my canoe, I feel somehow guilty at the intrusion of my paddle into the stillness of the lake, the splintering of this unbroken plane by the prow…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Made in the shade Bright sunlight makes silver salmon difficult to catch By KEN MARSH Anchorage Daily News Published: May 4, 2006 Last Modified: May 4, 2006 at 04:55 AM The silvers were so fresh and bright on an August evening that I almost missed seeing them. Their scales reflected the sunlight and made the fish appear translucent, almost invisible. Only their darker, distinctively squared tails against the sandy riverbed gave them away. Bunched up in a tight knot, the school held midstream, hugging the bottom. Imagine a shadow the size of a car top backed by a dozen or more gently undulating tails. Reaching them with a fly rod wasn't easy. Back-cast-snagging willows and alders grew thick along the banks. And the current where they held was swift and deep, forcing me to cast far upstream …

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Taking to the trail Mountain bikes help anglers quickly head to backcountry By DAVE ATCHESON Daily News correspondent Published: April 30, 2006 Last Modified: May 4, 2006 at 04:55 AM It's not difficult to distinguish the real bicyclist from the occasional fisherman seen riding the trails of Southcentral Alaska. For one thing, the fisherman won't be wearing spandex. It's also unlikely they will be riding along with an army of other mountain bikers or traveling at a ridiculously fast clip. No, the fisherman will probably be chugging along at a rather sluggish pace, with a strange array of equipment -- like rod tubes and wading boots -- haphazardly strapped to the bicycle. That's because it hardly matters to most fishermen what they look like, and they are hardly concerned with aerodynamics. …

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Fishing for fun: Kachemak Bay's waters are full of halibut and salmon By KEN MARSH Anchorage Daily News Published: April 21, 2005 Last Modified: June 28, 2005 at 12:40 PM Halibut derby • It will come as little surprise that the town that bills itself "the halibut capital of the world" also hosts the state's largest and longest-running halibut jackpot fishing derby. How big is it? Well, last summer Don Hankins of Sparks, Nev., entered a 352.6-pound monster and walked away with $51,298. That's a lot of money -- and a lot of halibut. Even the smallest of last year's derby's top five fish weighed a whopping 292 pounds. • Derby tickets are $10 per day. • To learn more about the Homer Jackpot Halibut Derby, contact the Homer Chamber of Commerce at P.O. Box 541, Homer 99603; phone 1-907-235-7740;…
